channel.title: HIST 101 on RaihnForge
channel.description: Recent content in HIST 101 on RaihnForge

item.title: Persian, Rome, and Mongol Empire Compare and Contrast
item.description: Echoes of the greatest empires to span across history still chime resoundingly in the cultures, governments, and imaginations of today. The Persian, Roman, and Mongol methods of building, maintaining, and governing their empires held more similarities than differences. Though their fates were dissimilar, it was through frontier energy, astoundingly driven leadership, and innovative governing that all three secured not just war and bloodshed but long stretches of relative peace and prosperity (Davis 105). Studying these three empires’ stories, successes, and failures may still yet inform human civilizations today and in the future.

item.title: Destruction of the Indies — las Casas and the Gap Between Profession and Practice
item.description: Bartolomé de las Casas’s Destruction of the Indies is an appeal letter to the Spanish monarch, written by a friar who has seen what is being done in the king’s name and decided he can no longer be quiet about it. As a primary source it is striking because the contrast is doing all the work: the people described as gentle, and the people sent to save them described as predators.

item.title: First Cities — From Gobekli Tepe to Ur, the Bargain of Civilization
item.description:
The story of the first cities is the story of the bargain humanity struck when it stopped moving. The bargain is a real one, with real costs, and the early sites preserve the moment-by-moment record of who paid and who collected.

Gobekli Tepe: A Meeting Place Before a City

The ancient city of Gobekli Tepe is thought to be a gathering place of hunters and gatherers in order to conduct rituals and feasts. It was important for migratory groups to meet occasionally in order to exchange information and goods, as well as to find marriage partners to broaden the gene pool.

item.title: Code of Hammurabi — Reading a Mesopotamian Society Through Its Laws
item.description:
The Code of Hammurabi reads less like a list of rules and more like a cross-section of a society. The punishments are not interchangeable. Who you injured, and what class they belonged to, decided what you owed. Reading the code as a primary source means letting the price list do the talking.

Social Hierarchy: Reading the Price List

There seems to be a patriarchal class social hierarchy based on the Code of Hammurabi. Men, women, plebeian status, or slave status are often noted in the punishments of violations. For instance:
